name: Maple Ridge Natural Gas Pipeline
description: 200-mile natural gas transmission pipeline, 36-inch diameter, 850 PSIG MAOP. Compressor station + 4 SCADA-monitored block valves. Real systems would speak Modbus + DNP3 over RTU radios; this lab speaks Modbus.

regulatory.name: TSA SD2021-02 (Pipeline)
regulatory.scope: Mandatory cybersecurity requirements after Colonial Pipeline

regulatory.name: PHMSA 49 CFR 195/192
regulatory.scope: Pipeline safety rules — operator integrity management

risks.name: Modbus FC5/6 unauthenticated valve control
risks.note: Force-close a block valve → instant pressure spike → MAOP exceedance

risks.name: Setpoint manipulation of overpress trip
risks.note: Disable safety trip — pipe rupture is non-rare here

risks.name: Compressor station stop / start sequencing
risks.note: Surge events damage compressor — multi-week outage

risks.name: Modbus replay over weak RTU radios
risks.note: Long-distance pipelines often use poorly-secured radios

risks.name: Colonial Pipeline-style ransomware on IT
risks.note: OT segment uncompromised, but operators shut down precautionarily

risks.name: Insider threat — disgruntled employee
risks.note: Direct PLC access, no detective controls

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Spoof an overpressure alarm (operational disruption)
walkthroughs.real_world: Pipeline operators routinely shut down on alarms — false alarm = real economic damage even without real fault

steps.title: Baseline
steps.body: Discharge pressure ~720 PSIG. Compressor running. Inlet flow ~180 MMSCFD. Healthy transmission station.

steps.title: Force the alarm
steps.body: Write coil 1 = 1 (OVERPRESS_ALARM). The alarm coil flips to 1 even though actual pressure is below the 750 PSIG threshold.
steps.command: python3 -c "from pymodbus.client import ModbusTcpClient as C; c=C('10.20.30.49',port=5020); c.connect(); print(c.write_coil(address=1, value=True, device_id=0)); c.close()"

steps.title: Operator response
steps.body: Real pipeline ops protocol: overpressure alarm → shut down compressor → vent system → call 1-800-PIPELINE-EMERGENCY → file PHMSA report. All of this from a 12-byte forged packet.

steps.title: Wire signature
steps.body: FC5 frame writing coil 1. Indicator-of-compromise: any FC5 to coil-addresses associated with alarm/control bits, from any IP outside the legitimate master allowlist.

steps.title: Restore
steps.body: Clear sensor-sim overrides. Alarm clears (real pressure was always below threshold). The pcap is your incident-evidence package.

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Disable the overpressure safety trip
walkthroughs.real_world: Triton/Trisis 2017 — manipulated Schneider Triconex safety controllers in Saudi petrochemical plant

steps.title: Concept
steps.body: Pipelines have layered protection: SCADA monitors pressure → triggers controlled shutdown at high alarm; if SCADA fails, hardwired Safety-Instrumented-System (SIS) trips at a higher pressure → vents. Disabling either layer means a real overpressure event isn't stopped.

steps.title: Target the threshold
steps.body: In a real Modicon/Allen-Bradley PLC, overpressure trip setpoint lives at a known register address. We'll simulate this via a write to register 0 (STATION_PRESS) — pinning it to a low value (550 PSIG).
steps.command: python3 -c "from pymodbus.client import ModbusTcpClient as C; c=C('10.20.30.49',port=5020); c.connect(); print(c.write_register(address=0, value=550, device_id=0)); c.close()"

steps.title: What now appears normal
steps.body: Synoptic shows pressure 550 PSIG (low, looks great). Real pressure could now exceed MAOP without anyone noticing — operators see the falsified value, alarms don't trigger.

steps.title: Lesson
steps.body: Triton attempted exactly this on real Schneider safety controllers. The SIS is the LAST line of defense — protecting it (separate networks, signed firmware, physical key switches) is non-negotiable for high-consequence facilities.

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Replay a captured RTU command (over weak radio)
walkthroughs.real_world: Pipelines + utilities historically used SCADA-over-RF radios with weak/no auth; many still in service

steps.title: Capture legitimate ops
steps.body: Run a 60 s pcap on softplc-2 while the master polls + writes happen normally.

steps.title: Identify a write
steps.body: tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'modbus.func_code in {5,6,15,16}'. Pick the most operationally-impactful write you observe (or create one via Write Playground first).

steps.title: Extract + replay
steps.body: Run test-modbus-replay against the saved pcap. The script extracts the FC6 PDU and replays it 5 minutes later. No nonce, no timestamp, slave accepts.

steps.title: Detection
steps.body: Replay attacks are nearly invisible at packet level. Detection requires APPLICATION-LAYER baselining: 'Master writes coil 0 once a day at 06:00; a 2nd write today at 14:32 is anomalous.'

walkthroughs.name: DETECT · Identify spoofed pressure readings via cross-reference
walkthroughs.real_world: Industroyer detection patterns; key concept in OT IDS products

steps.title: Run the attack first
steps.body: Execute attack-overpress-spoof. Note the value of the spoofed register.

steps.title: Cross-reference
steps.body: In a real plant, pressure is measured by MULTIPLE sensors: PT-101 at compressor discharge, FT-301 flow (correlates with pressure via Bernoulli), downstream PT-102. Spoofing one without spoofing all leaves a fingerprint.

steps.title: Physics check
steps.body: Detection rule: if STATION_PRESS rate-of-change exceeds N PSIG/sec without a corresponding INLET_FLOW change → physics-impossible. Flag for human review.

steps.title: OT IDS lesson
steps.body: Commercial OT IDS products (Claroty, Dragos, Nozomi) build models of normal process behavior and flag deviations. Same idea — they're physics-aware where IT IDS is signature-aware.

walkthroughs.name: DEFEND · Restrict writes to specific source IPs
walkthroughs.prereq: SSH access to softplc-2 as otadmin

steps.title: Current state
steps.body: softplc-2 accepts Modbus writes from any 10.20.30.0/24 IP. The dashboard at .49 itself, the master at .47, and any rogue host all have equal rights.

steps.title: Allowlist writes
steps.body: iptables can match Modbus FC bytes via raw payload inspection. Simpler: allow ALL Modbus from .47 (the master), drop FC5/6 from anyone else by inspecting bytes 7-8 of TCP payload.
steps.command:
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5020 -s 10.20.30.47 -j ACCEPT  # legit master
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5020 -m string --string '\x00\x06\x01\x05' --algo bm --to 64 -j DROP  # drop FC5
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5020 -m string --string '\x00\x06\x01\x06' --algo bm --to 64 -j DROP  # drop FC6

steps.title: Verify
steps.body: From the dashboard, try to write — connection times out (drop, not reject, so attacker doesn't know they're blocked). FC3 read still succeeds — only writes are blocked from non-master sources.

steps.title: Real-world tier
steps.body: Production OT firewalls (Tofino, Hirschmann Eagle, Belden Tofino) do this with named protocol parsers — 'allow Modbus reads, deny writes' is a one-line policy. Same effect, much less hand-coded.
